artifacts.content:
From: daniel.morales.sf@gmail.com
To: elena.ruiz.pdx@gmail.com
Subject: Portland budget spreadsheet v3
Date: 2024-10-09

Hey love,

Just finished another pass on the “Portland budget spreadsheet v3” and wanted to walk you through what I changed before you get home from the evening shift.

I added a separate tab for “transition months” (Dec–Feb) where I assumed zero income for me, just to be conservative. That’s what’s freaking me out a bit. I still have that remaining ~$11,000 in federal student loans from UC Davis hanging over me, and even on the new SAVE plan the payments aren’t nothing. I’m nervous about a few months without a paycheck after I leave Harborview on 11/30 and trying to keep up with those plus Portland rent.

The good news: between your salary, our joint savings, and the little emergency fund I’ve squirreled away, we’re not in disaster territory. But I do think we should be honest that dinners out and random Amazon stuff need to chill until I land something in Portland.

You’ll see comments in the sheet about the moving pod and deposits. I got a quote for around $3,200 total. That’s why I’m planning to call the credit card company from the office next week to ask for a temporary limit bump so we can put the pod + deposit on it and then pay it down once the dust settles. I hate the idea of leaning on credit while I’m technically “between jobs,” but I don’t see a cleaner way.

None of this changes the big picture: I still feel really strongly that getting out of the SF–PDX ping-pong is the right move, especially before we get to late 2025 and start trying for a baby like we’ve been talking about. I really don’t want to be that dad who’s always flying back and forth for work.

Anyway, take a look when you have a minute and drop comments in the sheet. We can screen-share this weekend and tweak.

Love you,
D

From: daniel.morales.sf@gmail.com
To: priya.narayanan@harborviewanalytics.com
Subject: Notes from 14B + random thoughts
Date: 2024-08-22

Hey Priya,

Just wanted to follow up after our handoff session today and also debrief a little.

First, you did great walking through the readmission pipeline for St. Helena and the retail churn model queries. I know it’s a lot to absorb, especially with the year-end reporting cycle looming, but you’re picking it up faster than I did back in 2020.

On a more personal note, I’m still turning over that 1:1 I had with Elaine in Conference Room 14B last week. I told her I was worried about being labeled “less committed” because I’d just declined those two new Bay Area client accounts that would have required me to be on-site four days a week. I literally said, “I’m trying to make this Portland move work without tanking my standing on the team.” She was understanding, but it still felt risky to be that honest.

It reminded me of that Zoom with Mark Reynolds back in July 2022 — remember the “Quick sync on client expectations” invite? That was when St. Helena Medical Group complained about the readmission-rate dashboard refresh being delayed because I was working remotely from Portland that week and couldn’t get to their Oakland office in person. It never became formal, but that informal warning really stuck with me and made me hyper-aware of how the hybrid thing can backfire.

I’m sharing this not to freak you out but so you have context. You’re inheriting some accounts where clients are used to me being the calm explainer on the phone, and sometimes in person. Don’t be afraid to push back on unreasonable in-person expectations, but also loop Elaine in early if anything starts to wobble.

If you ever want to talk through client politics or just vent, I’m around. Even after I’m gone, you can ping my personal email.

– Daniel

Title: Why I need Portland to work
Date: 2024-01-29
Location: SoMa studio, SF

I’m back from the Central Valley and still buzzing from that tense weekend at Mom and Dad’s.

Dad (Miguel) kept up the same script: comments about me being “too soft,” jokes about me “following Elena instead of being the man of the house.” It stung more than usual. I caught myself shrinking in conversations, changing the subject every time Portland came up. On the drive back up 580 I realized how much I’ve been underestimating the emotional drag of those visits.

Part of leaving San Francisco really is to create some distance from that. I want Portland to be a place where I’m building something with Elena and not constantly replaying Dad’s expectations in my head.

There are other layers too:

– In March 2023, after months of flying back and forth between SF and Portland, I did that telehealth appointment from the SoMa studio with Dr. Lauren Chen at UCSF Health. She prescribed a low-dose SSRI for anxiety and wrote a note recommending a more stable work-travel schedule. I remember closing the laptop and realizing the constant shuttle wasn’t just “a fun adventure” anymore; it was messing with my head.

– Last fall, walking along the Embarcadero while on the phone with Mom, I heard myself say out loud that Elena and I want to start trying for a baby in late 2025, and that I don’t want to be “the dad who’s always flying back and forth for work.” Mom immediately pivoted to how excited she is for grandkids, but for me it was more about not recreating Dad’s emotional distance in a different form.

So: Portland has to work because it represents a different pattern — proximity to Elena and Carla, saner travel, and a chance to set boundaries with Dad without cutting him off entirely.

metadata.title: Why I need Portland to work

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.morales.sf@gmail.com
To: diego.morales24@gmail.com
Subject: Re: Resume stuff + life update
Date: 2024-09-05

Hey bro,

Just read your draft resume — I left comments in Suggest mode, but overall it looks way stronger than the last version. Your bullet about managing the warehouse inventory system is actually impressive; don’t undersell it.

You asked how I knew it was time to really commit to Portland. Honestly, it wasn’t one big moment. It was a bunch of small ones:

– That Zoom in July 2022 with Mark (“Quick sync on client expectations”) when St. Helena Medical Group complained about the delayed readmission dashboard refresh because I was working remotely from Portland and couldn’t get to their Oakland office. It was only an informal warning, but it made me realize the hybrid thing was starting to crack.

– The telehealth with Dr. Lauren Chen at UCSF in March 2023, where she put me on a low-dose SSRI and literally wrote a note saying I needed a more stable work-travel schedule. I remember pacing around my SoMa studio thinking, “Okay, this isn’t just normal work stress anymore.”

– That walk along the Embarcadero last week when I called Mom. I told her that part of the urgency is that Elena and I have privately agreed to start trying for a baby in late 2025, and I don’t want to be “the dad who’s always flying back and forth for work.” Of course she immediately went into grandma mode.

On the flip side, Dad’s whole “you’re too soft” and “you’re just following Elena” routine after New Year’s pushed me further toward making my own call.

So when you’re thinking about your own next step, pay attention to those small signals. If your body and your boss and your family are all telling different stories, listen to the one that lets you sleep at night.

Let’s hop on a call this weekend and mock interview if you want.

Love,
D

Event: Call credit card re: moving expenses
Date: 2024-11-22
Time: 12:30–1:00 p.m.
Location: Phone booth, 17th floor, Harborview – Market Street
Attendees: Daniel

Notes:

– Step out after the 11:00 a.m. Tableau review with Elaine.
– Use the quiet phone booth on 17th so I don’t have to do this from my desk.
– Call credit card company and request a temporary increase in credit limit.
– Explain situation clearly: moving from SF to Portland, last day at Harborview is 11/30/24, will be between jobs for a bit but Elena and I have savings to back this up.
– Need enough room on the card to cover the ~$3,200 for the moving pod + deposits on the new Portland apartment.
– Ask about:
   • Whether the limit increase is temporary or permanent
   • Any impact on credit score
   • Timeline for when it takes effect (need to book pod by early next week)

Context to remember:

– This is part of the bigger Portland plan — consolidating the chaos of the SF–PDX back-and-forth, especially before we hit late 2025 and start trying for a baby.
– Keep this call factual; no need to overshare anxiety about my remaining student loans.
– After the call, update the “Portland budget spreadsheet v3” and drop a note to Elena so she can adjust the savings tab.
– Deep breath before dialing. This is just logistics, not a moral failing.

metadata.title: Call credit card re: moving expenses

artifacts.content:
Title: Jobs to research – non-solicit safe list
Date: 2024-05-07
Location: 845 Market St, desk

Goal: Map out Portland-area roles that won’t run afoul of Harborview’s non-solicit.

1. Providence Health – Portland analytics role
   – This is the one I Slacked Karen Liu about last week (confidential DM titled “Question about relocation and non-solicit”). The position is partly funded by that former healthcare client we did the readmission dashboards for, but Karen said the contract is technically with a different legal entity, so it shouldn’t violate anything.
   – Action: Okay to apply, but don’t pitch myself as “bringing Harborview secrets.” Focus on my experience with patient readmission and appointment no-show analytics in general.

2. Other health systems (Legacy, OHSU, etc.)
   – No direct overlap with my current book of business.

3. Retail analytics roles in PDX (New Seasons, Columbia, etc.)
   – Again, no conflict.

Context swirling in my head:

– The August 2024 1:1 in Conference Room 14B with Elaine is still ahead of me, but I can already feel the tension about being seen as “less committed” if I keep turning down Bay Area accounts that demand four days a week on-site. I want to make this Portland move work without tanking my standing on the team.

– That July 2022 Zoom with Mark (“Quick sync on client expectations”) after the St. Helena Medical Group dashboard delay was the first sign that the hybrid SF/Portland rhythm could hurt my reputation.

– Layer on top of that the March 2023 telehealth with Dr. Lauren Chen at UCSF where she put me on a low-dose SSRI and wrote a note recommending a more stable work-travel schedule, plus the conversation with Mom along the Embarcadero about Elena and me aiming to start trying for a baby in late 2025.

All of this pushes toward the same conclusion: find a Portland-based role that lets me stay in healthcare/retail analytics without dragging Harborview’s clients into any awkward non-solicit gray zones.

metadata.title: Jobs to research – non-solicit safe list

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.morales.sf@gmail.com
To: carla.morales.pdx@gmail.com
Subject: Re: Thanksgiving + moving circus
Date: 2024-11-18

Hey Carlita,

First of all, absolutely yes to me crashing on your couch if the moving pod is late. I’ll bring coffee and let Sofia cover me in stickers.

It’s been a weird emotional mix lately. On one hand, I’m excited to finally be in the same city as you, Elena, and the kids. On the other, I keep replaying little moments from the last few years.

Remember when I called you after that telehealth with Dr. Lauren Chen in March 2023? I was pacing around the SoMa studio while she prescribed a low-dose SSRI for anxiety and wrote a note saying my work-travel schedule needed to be more stable. That was the first time I admitted to myself that the constant SF–Portland flights weren’t sustainable.

Then there was the Zoom with Mark in July 2022 — “Quick sync on client expectations” — where he gave me that informal warning because St. Helena Medical Group was mad the readmission dashboard refresh got delayed when I was working remotely from Portland and couldn’t get to their Oakland office. It never went on paper, but it shook me.

Fast-forward to August this year, sitting in Conference Room 14B with Elaine, telling her I was worried about being seen as “less committed” because I’d turned down two new Bay Area accounts that needed four days a week on-site. I literally said, “I’m trying to make this Portland move work without tanking my standing on the team.”

Layer on Dad’s “you’re too soft” comments after New Year’s and that phone call with Mom along the Embarcadero where I told her Elena and I want to start trying for a baby in late 2025 and I don’t want to be “the dad who’s always flying back and forth for work.” It’s a lot.

Anyway, thanks for always listening to my rambles between Sofia’s snack emergencies. Can’t wait to be the local Tío Dani.

Besos,
D

Hi Elena,

I just wanted to take a moment amidst the chaos of packing to reflect on everything that's happening. It feels surreal that my last day at Harborview Analytics was just a couple of weeks ago on November 30th. I remember starting there on March 16, 2020, right at the onset of the pandemic! It was a bit nerve-wracking to onboard entirely over video calls, but looking back, it's incredible how much I've grown since then.

When I started as a Data Analyst, I was tasked with collecting, cleaning, and analyzing large datasets for our healthcare and retail clients. I built automated reports and interactive dashboards in Tableau and Power BI to track crucial KPIs like patient readmission rates and appointment no-show rates, which were essential during the pandemic. I also wrote SQL queries to extract and join data from our PostgreSQL and SQL Server databases and used Python (pandas, NumPy) for all sorts of analyses, including predictive modeling for churn risk scoring and demand forecasting.

I can't believe I started with a salary of $72,000 and ended up making $91,500 by the time I left. It was rewarding to mentor junior analysts and co-lead our monthly 'best practices in analytics' lunch-and-learn series. It feels good to know I left the team in a strong place.

Now, as I pack up my apartment here at 845 Market Street, I reminisce about the memories we've made in San Francisco. The walks along the Embarcadero and the late-night talks about our future plans are especially poignant now. Soon, I’ll be joining you in Portland, and I'm genuinely excited about setting up our new place—picking out a spot for my desk and deciding where to put the whiteboard. I’ve already started updating my resume and portfolio, tailoring them for analytics roles in Portland. I’m researching healthcare systems and retail companies there, hoping to find a good fit soon.

I know our parents are thrilled that we'll be closer to at least one side of the family. My mom, Rosa, will surely keep hinting about grandkids, especially now that Carla and Sofia are nearby. I’m really looking forward to this new chapter with you, even if it means navigating the rainy Portland winters.

Let's chat tonight about our plans for the apartment and maybe plan a visit to that gym we found online?

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Wrapping Up in SF and Looking Forward to Portland
